straight_beat.name: Casey Underwood
straight_beat.column: The Rockies lost again yesterday, 6-7 to the Astros, and now they’re 6-11, seven games behind in the NL West. That’s the numbers, but the real story isn’t in the scoreboard. It’s in the way the team keeps stumbling over the same rocks—pitching breakdown

optimist.name: Winnie Calderón
optimist.column:
The Rockies lost yesterday, but that’s not the story I’m telling. The story is about the hum of the stadium, the way the sun hits the outfield grass at 5:45 p.m., and the unshakable belief that this team is still here, still breathing, still chasing something bigger than a win. There’s a reason Coors Field feels like a cathedral to hope. It’s not just the altitude or the view of the city—it’s the way the game hums here, like a lullaby for the underdog. The Astros took it yesterday, sure, but the Rockies? They’re still in the game.  

You think the rotation is broken? You think the bats are dormant? You think the front office has given up? You’re wrong. The rotation’s a puzzle, yes, but puzzles have pieces that click when you least expect it. And the bats? They’re not just swinging at pitches—they’re swinging at possibilities. Every at

pessimist.name: Dwight Krenek
pessimist.column:
The Rockies lost again. Six to seven. Another game, another loss, another reminder that this team isn’t just struggling—it’s doing it in a way that feels scripted, like a bad reality TV show where the producers know exactly when to cut to commercial. The record’s 6-11, and they’re 7.0 games behind in the NL West. Not a typo. Not a rounding error. A full seven games. That’s not a gap; that’s a chasm. And yet, here we are, still talking about it like it’s a Tuesday.  

The Monfort method, as I’ve said before, is a thing. It’s not a strategy. It’s a philosophy. A way of thinking that prioritizes suites over starters, farm equipment over free agency, and short-term cash flow over long-term baseball logic. You can’t blame the players for trying to win. You can’t blame the fans for hoping the front office will finally act like adults. But you can blame the front office for acting like they’re still in the 1980s, when the only thing that mattered was selling concessions and keeping the lights on.  

Look, I get it. The Monforts are businessmen. They’re not here to play baseball; they’re here to make money. But baseball isn’t a business that can be run like a warehouse. You can’t build a winning team by focusing on parking lots and farm equipment. You need pitchers who can throw strikes, hitters who can hit pitches, and managers who can make decisions without consulting a spreadsheet. The Rockies have none of that. They have a front office that’s more interested in spreadsheet cells than in baseball IQ.  

The Astros, meanwhile, are doing things like drafting players, signing free agents, and actually building a team. They’re not just winning games—they’re building a culture. The Rockies? They’re still trying to figure out why their starting pitchers are giving up runs like it’s a game of Jenga. And every time they try to make a move, it’s either too late or too small. The trade deadline is coming, and I guarantee you, there won’t be a blockbuster deal. There’ll be a few minor-league signings and a lot of sighs.  

This isn’t about the players.
